retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: While hypertension often accompanies eclampsia and is considered a distinguishing feature of the condition, it is not always present; some patients may develop eclampsia without severe blood pressure elevation. Conversely, not all patients with preeclampsia with severe features develop eclampsia. [10] [11] Moreover, proteinuria, a standard marker of preeclampsia, may also be absent in some cases of eclampsia. Therefore, the clinical characteristics of eclampsia are not dependable indicators. Additionally, the absence of severe preeclampsia features in some patients with eclampsia challenges the common belief that preeclampsia will always worsen over time, eventually progressing to eclampsia. [2] Clinicians should be careful to consider differential diagnoses, including posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, that present similarly to eclampsia and require careful management, including blood pressure control and long-term follow-up. [2] [11] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome ," for more information on this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia is a severe pregnancy complication characterized by the onset of seizures in patients with hypertensive disorders. Eclampsia typically manifests through new-onset seizures, which can be tonic-clonic, focal, or multifocal; these seizures must occur in the absence of underlying causes such as epilepsy or cerebrovascular events. Eclampsia most frequently develops in the first 48 hours postpartum; however, this condition can arise before, during, or after labor. The highest risk of occurrence is within the first postpartum week. [1] In some cases, eclampsia may develop without preceding preeclampsia signs, such as hypertension or proteinuria. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia is a significant cause of maternal mortality. Seizures can lead to severe complications like maternal hypoxia, trauma, and aspiration pneumonia, though long-term neurological damage is rare. Some women may experience lasting cognitive impairments, particularly after recurrent seizures or untreated severe hypertension. [2] There is uncertainty whether the pathophysiology of antepartum eclampsia differs from that of postpartum eclampsia. In either case, the condition often presents with symptoms like severe headaches, visual disturbances, and altered mental status—but it can also occur suddenly and without warning. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension In Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, including chronic hypertension, with or without superimposed preeclampsia/eclampsia, gestational hypertension, HELLP syndrome, and preeclampsia with or without severe features or eclampsia, present a significant risk of morbidity to both mother and fetus. Although appropriate prenatal care with close observation to detect signs of pre-eclampsia and prompt delivery to reduce or avoid adverse effects have produced reduced morbidity and mortality, they still exist. While hypertension itself presents concerns during pregnancy, adverse effects from progression to pre-eclampsia/eclampsia present the primary concern. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia is a life-threatening complication of hypertensive disorders in pregnancy, characterized by the sudden onset of seizures in the absence of other neurological causes. This condition can occur before, during, or after labor—with the highest risk within the first postpartum week. While preeclampsia, marked by hypertension and proteinuria, often precedes eclampsia, it may also develop without prior warning signs. Symptoms such as severe headaches, visual disturbances, and altered mental status are common, though eclampsia can manifest suddenly. Immediate management, including seizure control with magnesium sulfate and timely delivery, is critical to preventing maternal and fetal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Although preeclampsia typically presents with a hallmark history and physical signs and symptoms, several atypical presentations exist. This section will review the most common presentations of preeclampsia and those that should prompt further investigation and diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with preeclampsia, prevalent historical indicators often include the emergence of a new-onset headache resistant to conventional medications, distinct from pre-existing headache conditions or migraines. Visual disturbances may accompany this complaint. Patients may report discomfort in the right upper quadrant or epigastric region, coupled with feelings of nausea or vomiting. Shortness of breath and an escalation in swelling, exceeding baseline pregnancy-related symptoms, may also be articulated by affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia, a severe complication of preeclampsia, has multiple underlying causes, with uteroplacental ischemia being the primary mechanism. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Preeclampsia ," for more information on preeclampsia. Uteroplacental ischemia involves an inadequate blood supply to the placenta, leading to its infarction and the release of toxic substances into the maternal bloodstream. These substances may trigger seizures, the hallmark of eclampsia. The role of uteroplacental ischemia is supported by various animal models that replicate preeclampsia-like symptoms, including hypertension and proteinuria, through induced reductions in uteroplacental blood flow. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of preeclampsia begins with early diagnosis and intervention, focusing on adequate blood pressure control and seizure prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Eclampsia(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: There may be a history of worsening headaches with vision changes such as blurred vision or “spots.” Hypertension and proteinuria may be present or absent. Impending eclampsia may have clonus. Eclampsia is a clinical diagnosis described by the occurrence of new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with preeclampsia; however, on occasion, it may be the first presentation of preeclampsia. Clinical findings may include posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (due to vasogenic edema predominantly localized in the posterior cerebral hemispheres), including headaches, confusion, visual symptoms, and seizure.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Psychosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-induced hypertension and stroke due to preeclampsia or eclampsia (CT/MRI to rule out stroke)

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common history findings in patients with preeclampsia are patient complaints of a new-onset headache not accountable by any other alternative diagnosis (ie, history of headaches or migraines) that is unresponsive to medication. This complaint may or may not be accompanied by additional complaints of visual disturbance. Patients may also endorse right upper quadrant or epigastric pain with associated nausea or vomiting. Shortness of breath and a perceived increase in swelling, both worsening from baseline pregnancy-related symptoms, may also be reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Eclamptic seizures can occur before, during, or after delivery, with a significant number occurring during the antepartum period. Postpartum eclampsia, particularly late-onset eclampsia, defined as seizures occurring more than 48 hours after delivery, can still pose a significant risk. A large portion of these seizures occurs in the first 7 to 10 days postpartum, often presenting with neurological symptoms, including headache. Postpartum headaches should be carefully evaluated, especially if accompanied by altered mental status, seizures, or visual disturbances, to rule out other severe conditions such as cerebral venous thrombosis or reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the early signs and symptoms of eclampsia, including new-onset seizures, severe headaches, and visual disturbances. Differentiate eclampsia from other neurological conditions such as epilepsy or cerebrovascular events. Implement evidence-based protocols for managing eclampsia, including the administration of magnesium sulfate. Develop and implement interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients with eclampsia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Evaluation -- Additional Diagnostic Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging studies, including chest x-rays and obstetric ultrasounds, can also be utilized to assess maternal pulmonary edema and fetal well-being, respectively. For patients with neurological symptoms, such as persistent headaches or vision changes despite treatment for hypertension, neuroimaging is recommended to rule out conditions like stroke or posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome. [11] [16] [10] Neurologic studies, including electroencephalography (EEG), computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and cerebral angiography may be conducted following seizures. EEGs in women with eclampsia frequently show abnormalities such as slow waves or spikes, typically in the occipital region.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Eclampsia(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia is a uniquely pregnancy-related disorder that manifests as a new onset of generalized tonic colonic seizures. It typically occurs after 20 weeks of concluded gestation, although it may occur sooner with plural gestations or molar pregnancies, and may additionally occur in the 6-week postpartum window. It represents the severe end of the preeclampsia spectrum. Preeclampsia spectrum includes symptoms of the central nervous system (CNS), for example, severe headaches or vision changes, and may involve hepatic abnormalities (such as elevated liver transaminases with right upper quadrant/epigastric discomfort), elevated blood pressures, and also may include thrombocytopenia, renal abnormalities, and pulmonary edema. In developed countries, resultant maternal mortality may be as high as 1.8%, and in developing countries, it may be as high as 14%. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: tumor of the placenta (ورم خبيث في المشيمة), and the aforementioned uterine atony and sitting delivery position. These obstetric entities are also reflected in Newborn Screening Terminology, which incorporates maternal conditions such as pre‑eclampsia and mild hyperemesis gravidarum, underscoring the link between maternal health surveillance and neonatal assessment. Clinical databases are associated with all of the above concepts, providing repositories for documentation of normal and complicated deliveries, early signs of hypertensive disorders, infectious complications, multiple‑gestation outcomes, and postpartum events, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care, safety surveillance, and research within obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 763
retrieved_docs.content: and risk factors of pre‑eclampsia, placental disorders, amniotic‑fluid deficiencies, uterine contractile events, and labor‑induction failures with both uncomplicated (normal) and high‑risk obstetric scenarios, including singleton and multiple gestations. Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2491
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is included within the standardized medical query “ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)”. The hypertension SMQ is classified as a member of the group of precursors to pre‑eclampsia (مقدمات تسمم الحمل). Consequently, essential hypertension, as part of the broader hypertension concept, is recognized as one of the clinical features that may precede or contribute to the development of pre‑eclampsia, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing essential hypertension in pregnant patients to reduce the risk of this obstetric complication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: labor‑complication term “مضاعفات المخاض.” Maternal conditions that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are organized under “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.” This includes spotting complicating pregnancy (unspecified trimester) and its early‑trimester manifestation as first‑trimester hemorrhage, as well as hypertension‑related disorders. Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium belongs to the broader class “disease of the circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium,” and is further related to the inverse condition “hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” underscoring hypertension’s central role among pregnancy‑associated circulatory disorders. Hypertensive disorders intersect with other parent categories such as “complications mainly related to pregnancy,” “early or threatened labor,” and “indications for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, serving as valuable resources for understanding and managing these entities. They are linked to مقدمات تسمم الحمل (early signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature placental separation), providing data that aid in early detection, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making for these serious pregnancy complications. In addition, databases are associated with تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (contraction of the pelvic inlet), offering information on uterine and pelvic dynamics that can influence labor progression and delivery planning. Finally, they are connected to gestation twin (twin pregnancy), supplying comprehensive records that support the monitoring of multiple fetuses, assessment of maternal‑fetal health, and coordination of specialized care. By integrating data across these conditions, medical databases enhance clinicians’ ability to deliver informed, evidence‑based obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 146
retrieved_docs.content: obstetric events. Antecedents of pre‑eclampsia and hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم) are included as maternal‑obstetric risk factors, with renal revascularization surgery (جراحة إعادة التوعي الكلوي) identified as a therapeutic intervention for hypertension related to renal arterial disease. This integrated knowledge base supports precise coding, epidemiologic surveillance, risk assessment, diagnosis, management pathways, and targeted newborn‑screening strategies for clinicians caring for pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2798
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension (SMQ) is recognized as a member of the precursors of pre‑eclampsia, indicating that elevated blood pressure is considered one of the early clinical features that can precede the development of pre‑eclampsia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium” encompasses a range of adverse conditions that can arise during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period, including the specific complication “Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” which is recognized as a distinct type of obstetric complication. Within this broader group, the subcategory “COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY” is positioned as a child of the overall complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that it focuses on adverse events that are primarily associated with the gestational phase itself. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of obstetric complications, linking general categories to more specific clinical presentations that are important for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in maternal health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 559
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompass several serious obstetric conditions, most notably Hemorrhage during pregnancy—including abruptio placenta and placenta previa—and Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a specific form of Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth and puerperium, representing a circulatory‑system disease that arises or worsens during gestation and the postpartum period. This hypertensive disorder is further classified under the broader label “hypertension complicating pregnancy; childbirth and the puerperium.” Recognizing that hemorrhagic events such as abruptio placenta and placenta previa, together with hypertensive disorders of the circulatory system, are key complications of pregnancy is essential for effective obstetric assessment, risk stratification, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 981
retrieved_docs.content: presentations (Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth AND/OR puerperium), and cervical anatomical abnormalities (Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium). Recognizing these interrelationships aids clinicians in categorizing, diagnosing, and managing the diverse complications that can arise during pregnancy, childbirth, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1617
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with مقدمات تسمم الحمل and الالتهابُ المشيماءِ والسَّلَى, indicating that these information systems are linked to the clinical contexts of pre‑eclampsia precursors and placental inflammation accompanied by sepsis. This association suggests that data repositories may be utilized to support the diagnosis, monitoring, and research of these obstetric complications, thereby enhancing patient care and informing clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 137
retrieved_docs.content: تحريض المخاض**, the child concept **تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم** (cervical laceration), **ورم دموي توليدي حوضي** (pelvic vascular tumor), and links to unspecified fetal malposition or malpresentation, reflecting its role in maternal management during the puerperium. In the pre‑eclampsia domain, **مقدمات تسمم الحمل** (precursors of pre‑eclampsia) is a parent concept that contains both **ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)** (hypertension) and the broader obstetric‑complication SMQ, thereby integrating hypertension and general pregnancy‑related risk factors as early contributors to pre‑eclampsia. **Newborn Screening Terminology** is defined as a subset of **مقدمات تسمم الحمل**, linking neonatal screening activities to early detection of pre‑eclampsia risk factors and encompassing **قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة** and the concept **plod**. **Normal pregnancy and/or delivery** is classified under multiple gestational categories, including **Zwangerschap als bijkomstige bevinding** (pregnancy as an incidental

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling) and to postpartum obstetric operation. The Newborn Screening Terminology subset incorporates concepts such as pre‑eclampsia, mild hyperemesis gravidarum, and other risk factors, highlighting their relevance for neonatal screening. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications and database linkages provide an integrated framework for prenatal risk assessment, labor and delivery management, postpartum care, and newborn screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1035
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension, represented by the standardized medical query “ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)”, is a central concept that connects several important clinical contexts. It is identified as a member of the group of conditions that precede or contribute to pre‑eclampsia (“مقدمات تسمم الحمل”), underscoring its relevance as a risk factor in pregnancy‑related hypertensive disorders. The same hypertension SMQ is also listed as a member of the set of indications for renal revascularization surgery (“جراحة إعادة التوعي الكلوي”), reflecting its role in the evaluation and management of renal vascular disease. Essential hypertension, a primary form of high blood pressure, explicitly includes the hypertension SMQ as one of its components, reinforcing the classification of the SMQ within the broader spectrum of essential hypertension. In addition, benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis—a form of chronic, non‑inflammatory arteriolar damage in the kidneys—is described as clinically similar to essential hypertension, indicating overlapping pathophysiological features and potential shared therapeutic considerations. Together, these relationships highlight how hypertension serves as a common thread linking pregnancy complications, renal vascular interventions, and renal arteriolar pathology, emphasizing its importance in comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2073
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. Recognizing that hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa fall under the broader category of pregnancy‑related complications highlights their interrelated nature and underscores the importance of prompt diagnosis and coordinated management in maternal care.
